text.title: kb-versioned-agent-memory-files Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for kb-versioned-agent-memory-files. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every project using agent-memory MUST keep exactly four files at .aidocs/memory/ (or .product/memory/ per per-project convention): decisions.md, patterns.md, mistakes.md, session.md. Each entry MUST be a Markdown section starting with a date in YYYY-MM-DD form, a one-line title, and at least one citation — a commit SHA, ticket URL, PR URL, or transcript hash. LLM agents MUST append; they MUST NOT edit or delete prior entries. A pre-commit hook MUST reject any diff to .aidocs/memory/*.md (except session.md) that removes lines, and MUST require at least one citation token (commit:, ticket:, pr:, or transcript:) per added entry.
rule.rationale: Append-only turns memory into an audit log: any future agent or reviewer can trace why a belief exists. Citations forbid hallucinated lore — every claim points to a verifiable source. The split prevents one of the four roles from cannibalising the others (the typical failure mode where mistakes.md becomes a dumping ground for every observation).

rule.statement: .aidocs/memory/session.md MUST be the ONLY memory file an LLM agent rewrites. The agent MUST overwrite session.md with the current session's task, step, and modified-files list at every checkpoint. Pre-commit MUST allow non-additive diffs to session.md and MUST forbid them on decisions.md, patterns.md, and mistakes.md. At session end (success or abort), if any state in session.md should persist, the agent MUST promote it to one of the long-term files via append (with citation), not by leaving it in session.md.
rule.rationale: Without an explicit scratchpad agents either pollute long-term memory with per-task state or invent new files ad-hoc; both failure modes are documented in published Cursor and Aider postmortems. The promote-on-end rule keeps the boundary one-way: long-term files only grow, and the only way knowledge enters them is a deliberate, citation-bearing append.
